attributes.stem:
A two-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent due to concerns about his speech development. He can say 'mama' and 'dada' but no other clear words. He does not combine words into phrases. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He makes good eye contact, follows simple commands, and plays appropriately with toys. He points to objects he wants. His hearing and vision appear normal. His physical examination, including neurological examination, is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure and review in six months
attributes.explanation:
This child presents with delayed speech development at age two years. While he demonstrates appropriate social interaction, play skills, and comprehension, his expressive language is significantly delayed for his age. By two years, children should typically have a vocabulary of at least 50 words and be using two-word phrases.

Referral for speech therapy assessment is appropriate as the first step. A speech pathologist can conduct a detailed assessment of both receptive and expressive language skills and provide early intervention if needed. Early intervention in speech delay has been shown to improve outcomes.

Reassurance and review in six months would not be appropriate given the significant delay in expressive language at this age. Waiting could potentially delay necessary intervention and impact the child's development.

Sign language training would be premature without first assessing the cause of the speech delay. This child shows good comprehension and no hearing concerns, making sign language unnecessary as a primary intervention.

Referral for autism assessment would not be the first step as this child demonstrates appropriate social interaction, eye contact, and play skills. While language delay can be a feature of autism spectrum disorder, this child does not show other concerning features such as restricted interests or repetitive behaviours.

Brain magnetic resonance imaging is not indicated as the initial investigation. The child has normal development in other domains and a normal neurological examination. Neuroimaging would only be considered if there were other neurological symptoms or signs present.

The differential diagnosis for speech delay includes developmental language disorder, hearing impairment, autism spectrum disorder, and general developmental delay. The presence of good social skills, normal play, and apparent normal hearing makes some of these less likely, but a comprehensive speech therapy assessment will help determine the specific nature of the delay and guide appropriate intervention.
learningPoints: Speech delay in children requires comprehensive developmental assessment including hearing, vision, and social interaction.
Absence of two-word phrases by age two years warrants referral for specialist assessment.
Red flag signs in speech delay include regression of previously acquired speech and lack of social interaction.
references: Liang WHK, Gn LWE, Tan YCD, Tan GH. Speech and language delay in children: a practical framework for primary care physicians. Singapore Med J. 2023 Dec;64(12):745-750. doi: 10.4103/singaporemedj.SMJ-2022-051. PMID: 38047330; PMCID: PMC10775292.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
